cross_connections.display_label: Cincinnati Semi-Colon Club hosted by her uncle Samuel Foote
cross_connections.description: Stowe frequently attended the Semi-Colon Club in Cincinnati hosted by her uncle Samuel Edmund Foote, where she was present while preparing to marry Calvin Stowe in 1836.

cross_connections.display_label: Born in Litchfield house Elijah Wadsworth built
cross_connections.description: Stowe was born in 1811 in the Litchfield house that Elijah Wadsworth built — the house was later acquired by her father Lyman Beecher.

cross_connections.display_label: Half-sister through Lyman Beecher; Isabella married a Hooker
cross_connections.description: Half-sisters through Lyman Beecher — Harriet by Roxanna Foote (1st wife), Isabella by Harriet Porter (2nd wife). Isabella married a direct descendant of Rev. Thomas Hooker.

cross_connections.display_label: Stowe and Mark Twain were Nook Farm neighbors
cross_connections.description: Harriet Beecher Stowe and Mark Twain were Nook Farm neighbors — she had lived there since 1864, a decade before Twain built his house nearby.

cross_connections.display_label: Katharine Seymour Day, her great-grandniece, preserved her Hartford house
cross_connections.description: Katharine Seymour Day was Stowe's great-grandniece, purchased her Hartford house in 1924, and established the foundation that preserves it today.

cross_connections.display_label: Defended Cooke's authorship against a Pennsylvania imposter

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Resident (1864-1896); Stowe lived at Nook Farm from 1864 — Mark Twain became neighbor in 1874. Her house preserved as the Harriet Beecher Stowe Center.

narrative_blocks.header: Sold 300,000 copies in its first year
narrative_blocks.body: Her 1852 novel about the cruelty of slavery sold over 300,000 copies in the United States in its first twelve months — proportionally equivalent to roughly thirty million copies today. It was translated into more than sixty languages and made her the most widely read American author in the world.

narrative_blocks.header: Wrote between housework and seven children
narrative_blocks.body: She drafted Uncle Tom's Cabin at her kitchen table while raising seven children and managing the household; the novel first appeared as a serial in the National Era because she lacked time for a complete manuscript. Her sister-in-law had dared her to 'write something that will make this whole nation feel.'

narrative_blocks.header: Born into America's most famous Protestant family
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Lyman Beecher was the leading evangelical preacher of his era; brother Henry Ward Beecher would become the most famous American Protestant minister of the nineteenth century, and sister Catharine Beecher pioneered women's education. The phrase 'Beecher's Bibles' — Sharps rifles smuggled to Bleeding Kansas — referred to shipments Henry organized.

narrative_blocks.header: Founded a Florida community for newly freed people
narrative_blocks.body: After the Civil War she purchased a plantation in Mandarin, Florida and converted part of it into a school and Episcopal church for the freedmen who lived nearby. She spent half of every year in Mandarin from 1867 until her husband's death in 1886.

narrative_blocks.header: Mark Twain's neighbor for twenty-three years
narrative_blocks.body: She lived at Forest and Farmington Avenues in Hartford from 1873 until her death; her house and Mark Twain's stand a hundred yards apart in the Nook Farm literary colony. Twain visited her often and was distressed by her mental decline in old age.
